title: Bordism of Flow Modules and Exact Lagrangians
speaker: Ivan Smith
abstract: We discuss constraints on exact Lagrangian embeddings obtained from considering bordism classes of flow modules over Lagrangian Floer flow categories. This talk reports on joint work with Noah Porcelli.

proof_stages.label: Floer equivalence implies ordinary homology equality
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from exact Lagrangians and recalls the standard mechanism by which a Fukaya-category equivalence, expressed by Floer cochains alpha and beta whose products are identities, gives a chain-level bordism/open-closed argument forcing the Lagrangians to represent the same ordinary homology class and hence motivating stronger bordism questions.

proof_stages.label: Spectral branes and framed Donaldson-Fukaya category
proof_stages.summary: Assuming TX is stably trivial as a complex bundle, the speaker defines spectral Lagrangian branes by nullhomotopies of the stable Gauss map to U/O and states the construction of a Donaldson-Fukaya-type category F(X;S) whose morphisms are framed-flow-module bordism classes and whose equivalences detect framed bordism classes.

proof_stages.label: Integral homology-sphere comparison theorem
proof_stages.summary: For spectral-brane Lagrangian integral homology spheres L and K that are equivalent in the ordinary integral Fukaya category, the announced topological conclusion is equality in framed bordism modulo the product by eta in Omega_1^fr(pt), in particular equality up to two-torsion.

proof_stages.label: Framed flow categories and flow modules
proof_stages.summary: The Floer data are encoded as framed flow categories with intersection points as objects and compactified strip moduli spaces as framed manifolds with corners; flow modules, their bordisms, and their composition provide the morphism objects used to define cochains in F(X;S).

proof_stages.label: Dimension truncations and obstruction theory
proof_stages.summary: The tower of tau<=k morphisms remembers only low-dimensional moduli spaces, with tau<=0 recovering ordinary chain-level Floer theory, and lifting a flow module from level k to k+1 is governed by an obstruction class in Morse homology of the relevant flow category with framed-bordism coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Obstruction calculation for homology spheres
proof_stages.summary: Starting with the integral quasi-isomorphism cochains for Lagrangian homology spheres, the obstruction groups vanish for degree reasons until a final possible obstruction in H_0 with coefficients in Omega_{n-1}^fr remains, interpretable as the stable homotopy class of an (n-1)-dimensional strip moduli space.

proof_stages.label: Baas-Sullivan singularities kill the remaining obstruction
proof_stages.summary: By allowing Baas-Sullivan singularities modeled on arbitrary (n-1)-dimensional framed manifolds, the last obstruction is set to zero, so the lifted equivalence exists in the singular version of the category and yields the stated quotient of framed bordism.

examples.name: Stabilized twice-generator circle in T^*T^2
examples.note: The attempted representative of twice the generator in C^* and its stabilization by an equator illustrates that smooth or totally real embeddings may exist even when Lagrangian embeddings are obstructed.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the stabilized circle in the product, explains how an isotopy in the second factor removes the self-intersection, and identifies the relevant homology class.

examples.name: Two-intersection Lagrangian in T^*S^n
examples.note: Taking the zero section and a Lagrangian meeting it in two points makes the abstract remaining obstruction visible as the framed bordism class of a single (n-1)-dimensional moduli space.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the two-intersection case by identifying the single moduli space, its dimension and framed bordism class, and the resulting Floer differential over the sphere spectrum.

passes.reason: Meeting the zero section in two points is the minimal/perfect-intersection case, and the resulting theta class is presented as an obstruction to spectral Floer equivalence.
The two-intersection setup is a minimal-intersection case whose single framed moduli space gives the obstruction theta to spectral Floer equivalence.
A Lagrangian meeting the zero-section in only two points is a minimal-intersection Floer model used to exhibit the possible spectral obstruction theta.
Having exactly two intersections with the zero section is a minimal Floer-theoretic case, used to display the surviving theta obstruction to spectral Floer equivalence.
A Lagrangian meeting the zero section in only two points is a minimal-intersection case and is used to exhibit the obstruction class theta.
The two-intersection setup is a minimal two-generator Floer test case where the framed moduli class theta obstructs spectral equivalence.
the two-intersection case is the minimal two-generator Floer situation and is used as the model obstruction theta to spectral Floer equivalence
The two-intersection setup displays the theta obstruction and is a minimal two-generator Floer configuration.
a Lagrangian meeting the zero section in only two points is a minimal-intersection model used to exhibit the theta obstruction to spectral Floer equivalence
two intersections with the zero section give the minimal Floer/Morse-type count for a sphere, but the example only illustrates the obstruction class
The two-intersection configuration is a minimal-intersection Floer model used to exhibit the theta obstruction to spectral quasi-isomorphism.
the two intersections give a minimal/perfect Floer configuration, and its framed moduli class theta is presented as the obstruction to spectral quasi-isomorphism

examples.name: Single-intersection open-closed bordism with product boundary terms
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker imagines alpha and beta supported at single intersection points to explain why extra product-type boundary strata disappear in an appropriate decomposable quotient of framed bordism.
examples.engagement_reason: In the Q&A the speaker runs the open-closed bordism picture in the single-intersection case and reasons through the extra product boundary components and the quotient by product classes.

passes.reason: The single-intersection hypothesis is a simplified collapsed case of the open-closed bordism argument, used to explain the quotient rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Morse-theory gluing-profile analogy
examples.note: An audience question about Morse trajectory spaces lets the speaker illustrate the smooth-manifold-with-corners issue by comparing different gluing profiles for broken flow lines.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through the Morse-theory analogy by explaining the gluing parameter, the choice of gluing profile, and how the smooth-corners structure succeeds or fails depending on that choice.

passes.reason: The Morse gluing-profile discussion is a standard analogy used to show that a naive smooth-corner structure can fail for a wrong natural parameter choice.
The Morse gluing-profile analogy is used to show that natural or wrong smooth-corner structures need not work.
The Morse gluing-profile case is an ordinary flow-line moduli-space analogy used to show that smooth corner structures do not follow automatically from a natural parametrization.
The ordinary Morse gluing-profile comparison is used to show that a natural or wrong smooth-corner structure on broken-trajectory compactifications can fail.
The Morse gluing-profile example is used to show that a natural naive smooth structure on compactified moduli spaces can fail.
